title: Asukas personality change after Instrumentality
wikitext:
[]: Asuka's personality change after Instrumentality             Quote Post by merri » Thu Sep 22, 2011 8:11 pm I don't understand why is seems to be common opinion that Asuka's personality "changed" after Instrumentality and resurrection. When she comes back to life, she is first seen catatonic on the ground, and only wakes from her state when Shinji starts strangling her, stroking his cheek and causing him to cry. If she had stroked his cheek and said nothing, or stroked his cheek and said anything positive, maybe that would be a better indicator that she has changed for the better. But by saying "Kimochi warui"-that's disgusting-in a very cold tone to a guy who has just tried to strangle her after seeing the gates of Hell, so to speak, does not present enough proof to say she has changed at all (in the original script what she says is even worse). So for someone who believes she changed and can change, please explain. I know this is dangerously close to fanwank territory, but I still find it interesting nonetheless. Also, if there is already a thread concerning this (and it hasn't been necro'd), I apologize. xD[/i]

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Sep 23, 2011 2:33 am Admittedly, I'm not in the majority, but as someone who thinks she's changed I base it on two things: 1. The root of her problems is that she thinks her mother abandoned her somehow, that her mother didn't want her. In EoE, she is reunited with her mother and learns that she never "abandoned her", she was there all along. I've referred to it before, and will again, as an epiphany. This, to me, is a huge step in her turning around. 2. Instrumentality is a very vague unexplained concept, but we do have a few clews to it's nature. Specifically, that Shinji went through it, and what he experienced. It seemed to be a sort of therapeutic dialogue, guiding Shinji along until he came to the conclusion that life is worth living, and/or that he's not worthless, and/or that people don't hate him (in the evaverse at least). I also believe in concurrency, which is the theory that the happenings of EoTV were, well, concurrent to EoE. That they were some subconscious or perhaps ignored part of EoE, and that Shinji's revelation into the "Congratulations" scene is the same revelation that led him to reject Instrumentality, we simply get to see it from his thought process in EoTV. If this is the case, then, since we see "The case of Asuka", she must have gone through a similar process (but not identical, because it's reasonable to believe it's been tailored to each person's problems). If so, she will, like Shinji, come out a changed person. But, like I said I'm not the majority, I'm probably a very small minority. Anyway, that's my thought process for the matter, I know others have different ones so I'll let them post theirs. Edit: I'm not sure it's as common as you think.

[]:             Quote Post by bobbyfischer's ghost » Fri Sep 23, 2011 7:55 am I think she did change just not in the way most would think, similar to how she changed after episode 22 of the tv series in that she could no longer run away from Shinji or her reality. the movie totally destroyed them mentally and know the both have to rebuild themselves from the ground up.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Fri Sep 23, 2011 9:21 am Asuka as she was during pre-Instrumentality would have just let him choke her, so clearly she's changed in some respect.

[]:             Quote Post by merri » Fri Sep 23, 2011 7:34 pm Azathoth wrote: Asuka as she was during pre-Instrumentality would have just let him choke her, so clearly she's changed in some respect.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Fri Sep 23, 2011 9:36 pm merri wrote: Not really. Maybe during her catatonia but after she wakes up and goes on psycho rampage against the MPEs she regains her will to live. Also, I doubt that even at her lowest she would let anyone kill her; that would be the ultimate submission, which she would never, ever give Shinji.

[]:             Quote Post by merri » Fri Sep 23, 2011 9:56 pm That can't really be used as evidence because there is no proof that scene actually occurs with Asuka; it occurs in Shinji's mind during pre-Instrumentality. At least, I don't believe that was the real Asuka. I think that's Shinji's mental model of Asuka, which in my opinion is the most credible interpretation. (This is also supported by Shinji's attempt to really strangle Asuka, as he might have wanted to see if this was the real Asuka) Also, if the director intended her to be willing to let Shinji really kill her, why was the original line she states at the ending (paraphrased) "I won't let you kill me". But regardless, that isn't really the topic.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Fri Sep 23, 2011 10:20 pm Shinji's "mental model" of Asuka is not meaningfully distinct from the real Asuka at that time, as ep25 says outright. That's what Instrumentality, and Shinji/Misato/Asuka's pre-Instrumentality, consist in: a merging of mental truth into external reality. Just because pre-Instrumentality occurs in Shinji's mind doesn't mean that it isn't also really occurring (in the sense that others are experiencing it as well). If you don't believe this is the case, then I'm really not sure what else to say to you.

[]:             Quote Post by SaVaGe » Fri Sep 23, 2011 10:31 pm I wouldn't expect her to come out of Instrumentality ranting and raving. She had just went through a psychological experience none of us could possibly relate to, and was just reborn into a completely unknown enviornment. Would you expect someone to come out of that as if nothing happened? Do I think she's going to be the exact same post-EoE? Of course not. Instrumentality would change everyone. But I find nothing in the final scene that lends itself to the fact that her personality had changed.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Fri Sep 23, 2011 10:53 pm She's making some effort to reach out to him, I guess. She could always have punched him in the nose. Merely the fact that she chose to exist signals some shift in her perspective on herself, though.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Fri Sep 23, 2011 11:00 pm Azathoth wrote: She could always have punched him in the nose.

[]:             Quote Post by merri » Fri Sep 23, 2011 11:24 pm esselfortium wrote: This would've absolutely been the best ending imaginable.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Sat Sep 24, 2011 12:25 am I think it is pretty important that she doesn't attempt violence against him. That's one possible irony of the scene: he tries to kill her because he knows they're alike; she tries to be kind to him because she knows the same thing. I'm not fond of interpretations of the characters which pigeonhole them into such and such a mental disorder. The tendency becomes to interpret all their behaviors as if they were prescribed by a specific set of rules of crazy, which isn't very sound psychological practice on a real person and is even less useful in finding out what is being expressed by the actions of a character. It would still have been pretty funny if she'd hit him, though.

[]:             Quote Post by merri » Sat Sep 24, 2011 12:58 am Azathoth wrote: I think it is pretty important that she doesn't attempt violence against him. That's one possible irony of the scene: he tries to kill her because he knows they're alike; she tries to be kind to him because she knows the same thing. I'm not fond of interpretations of the characters which pigeonhole them into such and such a mental disorder. The tendency becomes to interpret all their behaviors as if they were prescribed by a specific set of rules of crazy, which isn't very sound psychological practice on a real person and is even less useful in finding out what is being expressed by the actions of a character. It would still have been pretty funny if she'd hit him, though.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Sep 24, 2011 2:56 am Azathoth wrote: I think it is pretty important that she doesn't attempt violence against him. That's one possible irony of the scene: he tries to kill her because he knows they're alike; she tries to be kind to him because she knows the same thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Sep 24, 2011 5:20 am Azathoth wrote: It would still have been pretty funny if she'd hit him, though.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sat Sep 24, 2011 7:58 am Azathoth wrote: That's one possible irony of the scene: he tries to kill her because he knows they're alike; she tries to be kind to him because she knows the same thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Sat Sep 24, 2011 11:39 am Oh Tines, ever the optimist. CJD wrote: What do you mean by "He tries to kill her because he knows they're alike"?

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sat Sep 24, 2011 11:58 am Azathoth wrote: Oh Tines, ever the optimist. The idea would be that Shinji, in a state of despair (again) has decided by the beach scene that he doesn't want to exist in the new world. Still without the capacity to commit suicide, he decides to do the next best thing, doing Asuka (who he of course is well aware is "just like him" in some sense) a favor by knocking her off. Of course it's then hammered home to him that he's again falling into the pit of thinking that everyone thinks exactly the same way he does, than anyone is "just like him" - even the people who have similar problems to his own - and is in effect doing exactly the same thing that caused Instrumentality. So necessarily he freaks out and starts crying on her tits (making for a very amusing reverse of the scene in which he tells her that they're alike, no less). Asuka responds with a JC Denton-esque "what a shame". It's only a theory, of course, and doesn't entirely square with my own feelings on EoE, but I do like the element of symmetry with the "I get so angry whenever I even look at you" cut.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Sep 24, 2011 12:09 pm Azathoth wrote: Oh Tines, ever the optimist.
